Renaissance & Late Scholasticism

Nicholas of Cusa

1401 – 1464

Nicholas of Cusa can yield a genuine philosophy of mind, but only if we resist two reductions.

Nicholas of Cusa can yield a genuine philosophy of mind, but only if we resist two reductions. The first is the modernizing reduction that treats his God-language as ornamental metaphysics that can be discarded once we extract a theory of cognition. The second is the opposite reduction that treats his reflections on mind as merely devotional theology. In Cusa, “mind” is a finite, living image of Divine Mind; its activity is measuring, assimilating, judging, conceiving, numbering, and finally seeking its own measure in the infinite exemplar. Human mind, divine mind, angelic mind, animal discrimination, and bodily sensation are not separate topics but different levels in one noetic order.

The primary base for this report is Cusa’s own texts, especially Idiota de mente / The Layman on Mind, De docta ignorantia / On Learned Ignorance, De coniecturis / On Surmises, De visione Dei / The Vision of God, and the late Compendium. I have used the Cusanus Portal and the Jasper Hopkins translations, with attention to the portal’s critical-text framework and the fact that Hopkins’s complete translations are explicitly indexed there. I also checked Spanish, Italian, German, and French materials. The non-Anglophone reception is especially useful because it tends to emphasize the irreducibility, creativity, subjectivity, and embodiment of mens more than some narrowly epistemological English summaries do. For example, Spanish scholarship by Claudia D’Amico frames the Cusan doctrine of human spirit as a “metaphysics of subjectivity,” Ángel Luis González stresses the finite but active measuring power of mind, Italian work by Andrea Fiamma and Greta Venturelli highlights internal senses, spirits, embodiment, and the productive dynamism of mens viva imago Dei, French scholarship is careful about the non-identity of divine and human knowing, and German work emphasizes the passage beyond discursive ratio toward intellective freedom. Recent Anglophone-facing scholarship also increasingly recognizes that Cusa’s metaphysics can be read as a “metaphysics of mind” bound to mysticism and measurement rather than as a conventional substance ontology.

1. The central thesis: mind as living measure and image

Cusa’s most compressed definition appears near the start of De mente: mind is that from which the boundary and measurement of each thing derive, and mens is associated with mensurare, “to measure.” Immediately afterward he distinguishes mind from soul not as two substances but as two respects: mind considered in itself is mens; mind considered in its function of enlivening a body is anima. A created mind, insofar as it exists in itself, is either infinite or an image of the infinite; insofar as some such minds enliven human bodies, they are called souls.

This gives the first principle of a Cusan philosophy of mind: mind is not primarily a container of representations. It is a living measuring power. It measures not only by comparing external quantities but by setting boundaries, forming concepts, naming, proportioning, numbering, and making reality intelligible to itself. Yet this measuring is not sovereign in the modern constructivist sense. The human mind is itself measured by the Eternal Mind; it is a finite, created, “measured measure” that imitates the infinite measure without coinciding with it. This is why Cusa can say both that mind actively configures its world and that it remains dependent on an inaccessible divine exemplar.

The second principle follows: mind is living image. Human mind is the image of Infinite Mind, not merely a derivative copy. Cusa says that what is present in Divine Mind as precise truth is present in our mind as image, likeness, or conceptual presence; divine conceiving creates beings, while human conceiving assimilates beings. The difference is fundamental. Divine Mind is productive of reality; human mind is productive of notions, measures, concepts, sciences, arts, and conjectural worlds. Cusa’s philosophy of mind is therefore neither naïve realism nor idealism. The real world is from God; the rationally articulated world is from human mind’s assimilative activity.

2. The human mind: living substance, substantial form, and inner speech

In De mente V, Cusa calls mind a living substance that we experience as inwardly speaking and judging. In the present bodily state, mind’s function is to enliven the body, and in that respect it is called soul. It is a substantial form or substantial power that enfolds vegetative, sensitive, rational, intellectual, and “intellectible” powers.

This is a crucial anti-reductionist passage. Cusa does not treat intellect as a mere accident of the soul, nor does he reduce mind to a faculty added onto an otherwise independent animal organism. Mind is the living formal principle of the human. Yet it is also not a disembodied Cartesian ego temporarily attached to a machine. It is created in a body for the sake of actualization. Cusa’s preferred image is a divine seed placed in suitable earthly matter so that it can unfold conceptually the all-encompassing unity it virtually enfolds.

The human mind is internally unified but functionally many. Cusa describes it as a “virtual whole” composed of powers of understanding, reasoning, imagining, and perceiving. These are not external modules bolted together; each mode is in some sense the mind itself, as each part of a line is line. This is one of the strongest places to extract a Cusan theory of mental architecture: the faculties are modes of one living power, not isolated mechanisms. Perception, imagination, reason, and intellect are distinguishable operations of one mind-soul.

3. Divine Mind as archetype, not optional theology

The mind of God is indispensable to Cusa’s philosophy of mind because it supplies the archetype by which finite mind is intelligible. Divine Mind is Absolute Being and Infinite Truth; it contains things as their precise exemplars. Human mind contains them only conceptually or notionally, as likenesses. Cusa repeats the same contrast in De mente VII: Divine Mind creates by conceiving, whereas human mind assimilates by conceiving; Divine Mind is a “reifying” power, human mind an assimilative power.

That contrast yields an entire metaphysics of cognition. A finite mind can know because it is structurally akin to the divine exemplar, but it can never know with divine precision because it is image, not archetype. All human knowing is therefore both possible and limited. It is possible because the mind is not alien to being; it is an image of the Mind in which being is eternally conceived. It is limited because the likeness never becomes the infinite truth of which it is likeness.

Cusa also reads traditional “world-soul” and “nature” doctrines through this divine-mind framework. What Plato called world-soul and Aristotle called nature, Cusa says, should ultimately be understood as God working all things in all things, the Spirit of all things. He rejects the need for a subordinate cosmic executor because divine will and execution coincide in omnipotence. This matters for philosophy of mind because it prevents Cusa’s theory from becoming a demiurgic psychology in which “mind” is a cosmic middle-substance. There is Divine Mind, created minds, and the divine operation in things; but there is no independent world-soul into which individual minds dissolve.

Cusa also gives a trinitarian structure to mind. God’s understanding is not successive, and His understanding is His essence; it exists trinely because divine being, equality of being, and union are one. Human mind, as image of Eternal Mind, has an analogous triune structure: capability-to-be-assimilated, power-to-assimilate, and their union. Without such triune unity, Cusa says, our mind could not understand anything. This is not an incidental theological ornament. It is Cusa’s account of how understanding requires receptive possibility, active form-giving, and unifying completion.

4. No innate actual concepts, but an innate power of judgment

Cusa’s position on innateness is subtle. He rejects a simple Platonic theory of fully formed innate concepts forgotten because of embodiment. He also rejects a crude empiricism according to which the mind has no intrinsic norm by which it can learn. In De mente IV, the Layman sides partly with Aristotle: concepts are not concreated with the soul at the beginning. The soul needs the body, sense organs, perceptible objects, and perceptual images in order for its conceptual power to be stimulated and actualized.

Yet Cusa immediately adds that the mind must have a concreated power of judgment, otherwise learning would be impossible. It can judge rational considerations as weak, strong, or conclusive; it naturally judges the good, the just, and the true. The result is a powerful middle position: content is not innately possessed as ready-made concepts, but normativity is innate as a judging power. The mind does not begin with a library of ideas. It begins with a divinely given capacity to judge, assimilate, and form concepts when awakened by the world.

This is one of Cusa’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind. He gives us a model of cognition as activated potency. The mind has no actual concepts at first, but it has a concreated power by which it can become conceptually conformed to every form. Wonder and sensory encounter awaken the mind, which then discovers in itself the living description of eternal wisdom.

5. Cognition as assimilation: perception, imagination, reason, intellect

Cognition for Cusa is assimilatio: the mind makes itself like what it knows, but in different modes. In sight it assimilates itself to visible things; in hearing to audible things; in imagination to imaginable things; in reason to what is accessible by reasoning. This process involves bodily “spirits,” especially the spirit of the arteries, which Cusa uses to explain how sensory forms stimulate mental assimilation.

At the sensory level, the mind-soul configures subtle corporeal spirits in the organs according to the forms presented by objects. Imagination is a more general but less discrete configuration; reason gives clearer and more discrete configuration. These assimilations produce concepts of perceptible objects, from which the mind makes mechanical arts and empirical or logical conjectures.

Cusa’s account is therefore neither passive-reception empiricism nor pure intellectualism. The object contributes forms or likenesses; the body and sense organs mediate them; the mind actively configures, judges, and assimilates. Perception is already mind-involving in human beings. The Compendium’s theory of perception makes this explicit: in seeing, there are two likenesses, one of the object and one of the intellect. The object’s likeness is extrinsic and instrumental; the intellect’s likeness is intrinsic and formative. Perceiving requires both the sensitive soul and the perceptual form of the object.

Cusa also distinguishes conceiving from understanding. Mind is the power of conceiving; when stimulated, it moves through conceiving until it understands. Understanding is the completed movement of mind. Concepts, genera, species, differentiae, and likenesses are not inert contents but products of this movement.

6. Learned ignorance and conjecture: finite knowing as disciplined approximation

Cusa’s epistemology begins from the impossibility of exact finite comprehension. In De docta ignorantia, he argues that inquiry is comparative and proportional; the infinite as infinite is unknown because it escapes comparative relation. Number is the condition of comparison because comparison presupposes agreement, difference, and proportion. He later explains that measure and measured, however close, always remain different, and that the finite intellect can no more attain exact truth through likenesses than an inscribed polygon can become the circle merely by increasing its sides.

This becomes the doctrine of conjecture in De coniecturis. Since precise truth is unattainable, every human affirmation about the true is a conjecture; the growth of true apprehension is endless. That does not mean that all opinions are equal. Conjectures can be more or less adequate, clearer or more obscure, more or less proportioned to the truth. Cusa is not a skeptic; he is an anti-precisian. Human knowing is real, but it is finite approximation.

Mathematics has a privileged role because mathematical objects are relatively stable, abstract, and certain for us. Cusa says visible things are images of invisible things, and that spiritual matters unattainable in themselves are investigated symbolically; mathematics supplies especially fixed images for this ascent. So the Cusan mind is mathematical not merely because it calculates but because it measures, proportionates, symbolizes, and knows by ascending from finite signs toward the infinite.

7. The mind as producer of a rational world

De coniecturis gives the strongest “constructive” formulation. Surmises originate from our minds as the real world originates from Infinite Divine Reason. The human mind, as a lofty likeness of God, participates in the fruitfulness of creating nature by producing rational entities in the likeness of real entities. Cusa then states that human mind is the form of a surmised rational world just as Divine Mind is the form of the real world. The rational world unfolds from the enfolding mind, and the mind becomes more fruitful by contemplating itself in and through the world it unfolds.

This is perhaps the central text for extracting a philosophy of mind. Mind is not merely in the world; it forms a world of intelligibility. It produces rational entities, measures, numbers, names, arts, and sciences. Yet its productivity is analogical and subordinate: God creates real beings; human mind creates rational being, notional being, symbolic order.

Number is the chief instrument of this production. Cusa says number is reason unfolded, and creatures lacking mind do not number. In using number to make conjectures, reason uses itself and fashions things in its own likeness, analogously to the way Infinite Mind communicates being through the Word. This gives a deep philosophy of mathematics: number is not merely a property found in objects, nor merely a human fiction. It is the unfolding of rational mind’s measuring power, rooted ultimately in divine oneness.

8. Self-knowledge: the mind measures things in order to find its own measure

Cusa gives an elegant account of self-knowledge in De mente IX. The mind eagerly measures things in order to attain the measure of itself. It is a living measure that attains its own capability by measuring other things. It performs all its operations to know itself, but finds its precise truth only where all things are one, that is, in the adequate exemplar.

This is not modern introspection. The mind does not simply look inward and discover transparent mental data. It discovers itself by measuring the world, producing concepts, and recognizing the insufficiency of every finite measure. The self is known only through its relation to the infinite exemplar. This means Cusan self-knowledge is simultaneously epistemological, metaphysical, and theological.

A modern reconstruction might say: the mind’s self-model is generated through its world-model, but the adequacy of both depends on a transcendent measure. That is not Cusa’s language, but it captures the structure. The mind knows itself by seeing what it can do—measure, name, conceive, judge, symbolize, number—and then by realizing that its own measure is not contained within any finite product of those activities.

9. Signs, language, and the cosmographer model

The late Compendium intensifies Cusa’s semiotic philosophy of mind. Human beings form words not as brute animals do but as animals with mind. A word is a disclosure of mind; the mind’s conception of itself is an inner word, and vocal words disclose this conceptual word. Cusa then analogizes this to the divine Word: creatures are signs of the Uncreated Word, and the Creator reveals Himself in various signs.

The famous cosmographer image develops this into a theory of embodied cognition. A fully developed animal possessing both sense and intellect is like a geographer living in a city with five gates, the five senses. Messengers enter through these gates with reports about light, color, sound, odor, flavor, heat, cold, and other sensible qualities. If a gate remains closed, the world-description is defective; the geographer therefore tries to keep all gates open and make his description more accurate.

The metaphor is philosophically rich. The mind is not a passive spectator but a cartographer. The senses do not deliver a finished world; they deliver reports. The intellect organizes, orders, measures, and maps. The resulting cognitive world is a constructed description, yet it is constrained by sensory reports and ordered toward the real. This anticipates later themes in philosophy of mind—active perception, cognitive mapping, sign-mediated knowledge—without becoming modern representationalism in a simple sense.

Cusa also says that the object of mental sight and perceptual sight is one object: mental sight sees it as it is in itself, while perceptual sight sees it as present in signs. This is a powerful semiotic ontology. Sensible things are not distractions from truth; they are signs. But the mind must not stop at signs. It must ascend from sign to intelligible object, and from intelligible object toward the divine ground of intelligibility.

10. Mind and body: hylomorphic, instrumental, and survivalist

Cusa’s mind-body doctrine is best described as hylomorphic and spiritual, not Cartesian. The mind is the substantial form that enlivens the body, but it is also a living intellectual substance whose being is not exhausted by bodily functioning. In the embodied state, it needs the body for stimulation, sense images, and conceptual actualization.

The glass and cithara analogies are especially revealing. Cusa describes a glass that sounds when struck; when the glass breaks, the sound ceases, but he asks us to imagine that the power causing motion and harmony did not depend on the glass and therefore did not cease. Likewise, the art of cithara playing is not destroyed when a particular cithara breaks. The body is thus an instrument of mental operation in the present world, but the mind’s power is not reducible to the instrument.

This helps avoid two misleading readings. Cusa is not a materialist: mind is not simply a bodily process. But he is also not a simple disembodiment theorist: the body is not irrelevant, and the mind’s development in this life depends on sensory and bodily mediation. Human cognition is embodied without being ontologically reducible to embodiment.

11. Animals: discrimination without intellective mind

Cusa does not deny cognition to animals. He explicitly grants brute animals a discriminating power of inference: dogs recognize their master, seek him out, and respond to his call. But he distinguishes this discriminating animal power from human mind. Human mind is the higher form and perfection of animal discrimination, just as animal discrimination perfects mere sight.

This produces a graded cognitive hierarchy. Animals have perception, discrimination, and a kind of confused assimilation. They can form practical recognitions and inferential responses. But they lack mens in the strict sense: they do not number, do not form the rational world as mind does, and do not possess the intellective orientation toward truth, self-measure, and the infinite exemplar.

For a modern philosophy of mind, Cusa’s view is neither behaviorist nor flatly anthropocentric. It is hierarchical and perfectionist: animal cognition is real, but human cognition crosses a threshold when discrimination becomes intellective, symbolic, mathematical, self-reflective, and theologically oriented.

12. Individual minds: against the common intellect and against merger into world-soul

Cusa explicitly rejects the thesis that there is one common intellect in all human beings. Because mind functions as soul only through a suitable relation to a proportioned body, and because this proportion is not replicable, there cannot be an identity of mind across persons. Your seeing, your discrimination, and your understanding cannot be someone else’s.

He also rejects the idea that souls merge into a common world-soul after death. Even when material variability is removed and we cannot count separated minds as we count material things, plurality does not cease. It remains a number in the Divine Mind, though not a number countable by us.

This is important for personal identity. Cusa’s individual mind is not an isolated atom, because every mind is an image of Infinite Mind and exists in relation to the divine exemplar. But neither is it dissolved into a collective intellect. Personal distinctness is real, grounded in the unique proportion of mind and body in this life and in divine knowledge beyond bodily life.

13. Angelic minds and disembodied cognition

Cusa treats angelic or heavenly minds as a serious part of the noetic hierarchy. He speaks of intellectible, intelligential, and rational ranks, arranged in nine gradations or choirs. Human minds are below the first gradation of such spirits but above corporeal natures. They are the terminal perfection of lower natures and the beginning of the perfection of higher natures.

The crucial difference concerns stability of concepts. Blessed spirits existing apart from bodily instability have invariable concepts, not erased by forgetfulness, because truth is continuously present to them as object. Human minds, by contrast, forget because they are distracted by bodily conditions, neglect, inattention, and the variability of the world. Yet they retain a concreated aptitude for relearning, and concepts acquired in this world can pass into stable mastery in the invariable world. Cusa strikingly says that here we are learners, whereas in the next world we are “master-teachers.”

This angelology is not a decorative medieval add-on. It clarifies Cusa’s taxonomy of cognition. Animal cognition is sensory-discriminative; human cognition is embodied, conjectural, and progressive; angelic cognition is intellectual and stable; divine cognition is absolute, creative, and non-successive. Human mind is liminal: it is not angelic, because it depends on bodily stimulation in this life; but it is not merely animal, because it is intellective, self-measuring, and capable of stable truth.

14. Immortality of mind

Cusa argues for the mind’s immortality by several converging routes. One route is intellectual: the mind attains invariable truths within itself and is therefore not of the same nature as variable things known through the senses. Another route is numerical: mind is a living number that numbers; number in itself is incorruptible, and the author of number cannot be conceived as corruptible in the way numbered material things are.

He also argues from self-motion and enfolding/unfolding. Mind is an intellectual life that moves itself, generates rational operations from itself, enfolds while unfolding, and is an image of eternity. Time does not measure mind in the ultimate sense; rather, mind measures motion and time. Finally, Cusa says that only Uncreated Mind measures and bounds created mind, as Truth bounds its living image.

Whether one accepts these arguments or not, they reveal the structure of Cusa’s position. The mind survives because its essence is not bodily extension, sensory flux, or temporal succession. Its essence is living intellectual measure, image of eternity, and self-moving rational power.

15. The divine gaze and the phenomenology of being seen

De visione Dei adds a distinctively contemplative but philosophically rich theory of mind. Cusa’s icon of the all-seeing face is not just devotional imagery. It is a meditation on subjectivity, objectivity, attention, and divine omnivision. Cusa says God’s true Face is seen not by bodily eyes but by mental and intellectual eyes; the Face is free from quantitative, qualitative, temporal, and spatial contraction; it is the Truth and adequate Measure of all faces. Every face that looks upon God’s Face sees its own truth, because the divine Face is the exemplar of every face.

The finite mind’s highest encounter is paradoxical. To seek God’s Face unveiledly, the mind must pass beyond knowledge and conception into darkness or learned ignorance. Like an eye trying to see the sun’s light directly, it must pass beyond all visible light; the darkness produced by excess light becomes the sign of approach.

This has implications for philosophy of mind. The highest object of mind is not an object among objects. The mind’s relation to God is not ordinary representation but a transformation of seeing itself. The finite subject discovers that it is always already seen by the divine gaze. Cusa’s “I see myself seen” structure, to use later phenomenological language cautiously, makes subjectivity relational at its root: the mind is not merely a knower looking outward but a living image whose truth is held in the divine look.

16. Christ as the limit case of human and divine cognition

Cusa’s Christology also belongs to his philosophy of mind. In De visione Dei XXII, he describes Jesus’ human vision as finite and organ-bound, yet united with absolute and infinite Vision. With human eyes, Jesus sees visible accidents; with divine gaze, He sees the substance and quiddity of things. Cusa explicitly distinguishes ordinary human intellectual power, which is united to animal seeing so that humans discern and judge, from Christ’s unique union of human intellectual seeing with absolute divine sight.

The next page clarifies the general anthropology: human intellect is freer than sensible nature because it is not restricted to a bodily organ in the same way sight is restricted to the eye; nevertheless, human intellect needs images, and images require senses and body. Divine intellect, by contrast, depends on nothing, while all else depends on it.

This passage gives Cusa a layered model of cognition: animal seeing, human intellectual discrimination, angelic/intellectual cognition, divine vision, and the Christological union of human intellectual nature with divine nature. The Christological case is not reducible to psychology, but it helps define the boundaries of Cusa’s psychology: ordinary human mind is image-dependent and body-mediated; divine mind is absolute and unconditioned; Christ uniquely unites the two without collapsing finite into infinite.

17. Will, affect, normativity, and the ethical mind

Cusa’s mind is not a purely theoretical faculty. In De mente XIII, the mind as living image of Infinite Art has power, wisdom, and will in the oneness of its essence; it can become ever more conformed to Divine Actuality, while the precision of Infinite Art remains inaccessible. In the Compendium, he says the object of every cognitive power is equality, and that the human mind is a living intelligent manifestation of Equality. From this, he derives natural knowledge of the good, the equal, the just, and the right, including praise for the Golden Rule. Virtues nourish intellectual life.

This is a significant point for philosophy of mind. Cusan mind is intrinsically normative. It is not merely a computational or representational system. It is ordered to truth, equality, justice, and goodness. Its judgments are not only logical but ethical and spiritual. The mind’s desire for truth, awakened by wonder, is part of its very being. In De docta ignorantia, wonder precedes the desire to know, and the intellect perfects itself through the study of truth.

18. A reconstructed Cusan philosophy of mind

The following theses extract a systematic philosophy of mind from the texts without detaching them from their theological architecture.

First, mind is living measure. It is called mens because it measures, bounds, numbers, proportions, names, and makes reality intelligible. Its activity is not passive reception but active measuring.

Second, mind and soul are the same reality under different respects. Mind in itself is mens; mind functioning in and through a living body is anima. This makes Cusa neither a Cartesian dualist nor a materialist.

Third, the human mind is a living image of Divine Mind. This image-status grounds both its dignity and its limitation. It can assimilate all things notionally, but it cannot create beings as God does.

Fourth, Divine Mind is the necessary archetype of all finite mind. God’s conceiving is creation; human conceiving is assimilation. A Cusan philosophy of mind that removes Divine Mind loses the principle that makes finite mind intelligible.

Fifth, the mind has no innate actual stock of concepts, but it has an innate power of judgment. Cusa is anti-nativist about ready-made content but nativist about the normative capacity to judge, learn, and seek truth.

Sixth, cognition is assimilation. Sense, imagination, reason, and intellect are different modes by which mind conforms itself to what is knowable.

Seventh, the faculties are modes of one virtual whole. Perceiving, imagining, reasoning, and understanding are not separate substances but substantial modes of one living mind.

Eighth, human cognition is embodied in its development. In this life the mind needs perceptual images, bodily organs, and sensory stimulation. The body is an instrument of cognitive actualization, not an accidental prison.

Ninth, human cognition is not reducible to embodiment. The mind’s intellectual life, self-motion, relation to number, and orientation to invariable truth ground its incorruptibility and survival beyond bodily dissolution.

Tenth, all finite knowledge is conjectural. Human affirmations about truth are approximations. This is not skepticism but disciplined learned ignorance: knowing that exact truth exceeds finite comprehension.

Eleventh, mathematics and number are privileged because they express reason’s own measuring structure. Number is reason unfolded, and mind is living number.

Twelfth, mind produces a rational world. The real world originates from Divine Reason; the conjectural world originates from human mind. This is a theory of culture, science, naming, art, and symbolic order.

Thirteenth, self-knowledge is mediated by world-knowledge and completed only in relation to God. The mind measures things to find its own measure, but it finds that measure only where all things are one.

Fourteenth, perception is semiotic and active. The senses are gates; signs enter; the mind is a cosmographer who organizes reports into a map of the perceptible world.

Fifteenth, animal cognition is real but sub-intellective. Animals discriminate and infer, but they lack mens in the full sense: they do not number, form rational worlds, or seek their own measure in the infinite.

Sixteenth, individual minds are irreducible. Cusa rejects a common intellect and rejects postmortem merger into a world-soul. Personal plurality remains real, though separated minds are not countable by us as material things are.

Seventeenth, angelic minds are higher comparative cases. They show what stable, non-bodily intellectual cognition can mean. Human minds are below angelic spirits but above corporeal natures, standing at the threshold between the sensible and the intelligible.

Eighteenth, the mind’s final orientation is contemplative and transformative. It seeks the divine Face, but the divine Face is not an object captured by representation. The highest cognition passes through learned ignorance, where excess light appears as darkness.

19. What Cusa contributes to contemporary philosophy of mind

Cusa’s philosophy of mind is not modern philosophy in medieval dress. It should not be forced into current categories. But it does offer a coherent alternative to several modern binaries.

Against reductionist physicalism, he argues that mind is not exhausted by bodily mechanism, even though bodily mediation is essential for human learning. Against disembodied intellectualism, he insists that human concepts are awakened through sense, image, body, and worldly encounter. Against representational passivity, he portrays mind as active assimilation, measurement, and world-articulation. Against subjectivist constructivism, he roots mental production in the divine exemplar and in a real world created by Divine Mind. Against skepticism, he allows ordered approximation; against dogmatic rationalism, he insists on learned ignorance.

His view is also strikingly relational. Human mind is related downward to body and sense, laterally to signs, language, culture, and other minds, upward to angels and divine mind, and inward to its own self-measuring activity. The mind is never an isolated point. It is a living image whose activity is to unfold a rational world while being drawn beyond every finite world toward its infinite measure.

The resulting philosophy of mind may be summarized in one formula: for Nicholas of Cusa, mind is the finite living image of Infinite Mind, created in bodily proportion, awakened by sensory signs, productive of conjectural worlds, self-knowing through measurement, perfected by ascent from ratio to intellectus, and ultimately bounded only by the divine Truth whose image it is.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
